metadata.summary: Form and evaluate 3-5 change options plus status-quo against gap-priority map; score by strategic fit, cost, time-to-value, risk, and capability; hand selection to decision-analysis methodology.

content.title: Strategy Analysis: Change Strategy Formation
content.summary: The change strategy determines how to close the prioritized gaps from the gap analysis. It generates 3-5 options (build, buy, partner, modify, combination) plus a status-quo baseline, profiles each against cost / time / risk / capability criteria, and hands the option set to decision-analysis for weighted-matrix selection. The BA owns option generation and profiling; the decision-maker signs the recommendation before downstream SDD work begins.

why.p: Change strategy as a single option — "build it ourselves" appearing as the only path — is the third most common failure mode of strategy analysis. Buy, partner, status-quo, and phased approaches never get evaluated. The README mandates options; agents skip this when rushed or when the sponsor signals a preference early.

Without a status-quo option scored honestly, leadership cannot see the cost of inaction; the strategy reads as advocacy for the recommended option. The status-quo must be option zero with full cost/time/risk profiling — including the cost of NOT acting.

Without a pre-mortem, the risks section is written after the recommendation is made, producing a list that supports the chosen option rather than challenging it. A pre-mortem ("in 12 months we regret picking this — why?") surfaces structural risks before sign-off.

Strategy drift after sign-off is endemic: six months in, scope mutates and the strategy document is never re-opened. Without a change log plus quarterly review the rationale rots and downstream teams lose the "why."

list.item: After gap analysis is complete and prioritized — change strategy formation requires a signed gap analysis as input.
Any initiative where the business case will gate a significant spend — stakeholders need to see that alternatives were evaluated, not just the preferred option.
Portfolio planning where multiple options across multiple initiatives compete for the same budget envelope — the evaluation matrix enables cross-initiative comparison.
M&A integration planning: Day-1 / Day-100 / Day-365 plan options evaluated against the gap-priority map.
Regulatory-driven change: the change strategy documents how compliance gaps will be closed and justifies the chosen path to auditors.
Before gap analysis is signed — options cannot be evaluated without a prioritized gap list; the evaluation matrix has no rows to score against.
When the decision-maker has already committed to a path publicly — document it as a directive; producing a multi-option analysis becomes theatre and damages BA credibility.
Single-option technical fixes with an obvious solution and negligible blast radius — overhead exceeds value.

section.name: option-generation
section.p: The standard option types for change strategy are: build new capability in-house, buy a solution (SaaS or packaged), partner with a third party, modify existing assets, combination approach (e.g. buy platform + build integrations), and status-quo / do nothing. Every change strategy MUST evaluate at least 3 options plus status-quo.

rule.statement: Change strategy MUST evaluate a minimum of 3 substantive options plus the status-quo baseline. Strategies with fewer than 3 options plus status-quo MUST be returned to the change-strategy-agent for option expansion. The option count cap is 5 plus status-quo; more than 5 collapses into noise.
rule.rationale: Fewer than 3 options is a coronation of the preferred choice. More than 5 collapses decision quality. The status-quo documents the cost of inaction, which leadership needs to authorize spending.

rule.statement: Option order in every evaluation prompt MUST be randomized. The change-strategy-agent MUST NOT list the recommended option first or last without randomization; order bias causes the agent to favor whichever option is mentioned first or last in the narrative.
rule.rationale: Agents favor first/last position in option lists. Randomizing order before evaluation and re-randomizing on re-runs prevents systematic bias toward the "obvious" choice.

section.name: evaluation-matrix
section.p: Score each option against the evaluation criteria using a weighted matrix. Standard criteria and weights: Strategic Fit 25%, Cost 20%, Time to Value 20%, Risk 15%, Capability 20%. Weights are adjustable per initiative but must sum to 100%. Each score is 1-5; total = sum of (weight x score).

example.title: Change strategy evaluation matrix
example.code:
Initiative: Digital Order Management Transformation
Criterion | Weight | Build | Buy SaaS | Partner | Status-quo ------------------|--------|--------|----------|---------|------------ Strategic Fit | 25% | 4 | 4 | 3 | 1 Cost | 20% | 2 | 4 | 3 | 5 Time to Value | 20% | 2 | 5 | 3 | 5 Risk | 15% | 3 | 4 | 3 | 2 Capability | 20% | 3 | 4 | 4 | 1 Weighted Total | 100% | 2.85 | 4.20 | 3.20 | 2.80
Recommendation: Buy SaaS (score 4.20 of 5) Rationale: Highest strategic fit at lowest implementation risk; 3 qualified vendors identified. Status-quo scores 2.80 but carries a risk score of 2 (regulatory exposure escalates in 18 months).
example.note: Status-quo is scored as option zero. Its risk score of 2 documents the cost of inaction. The recommendation follows from the matrix, not from preference.

antipattern.description: Change strategy with a single option presented as the recommendation, with a section titled "Alternatives Considered" that lists two options with one-sentence dismissals.
antipattern.reason: One-sentence dismissals signal the alternatives were not seriously evaluated. Each option must be profiled against all criteria; the matrix does not lie.

section.name: risk-and-premortem
section.p: Before sign-off, run a pre-mortem on the recommended option: "In 12 months we regret picking this — why?" Answers feed the Risks section. Risk items discovered in the pre-mortem must be added to the change strategy before the decision-maker signs.

Schedule a 90-day and 365-day review at the time of sign-off. The BA and faion-improver compare predicted gaps closed vs. actual; calibration errors are logged in .aidocs/memory/patterns.md. This turns the change strategy from a one-time document into a learning loop.

rule.statement: The change strategy MUST include a scored status-quo option. The status-quo option MUST carry a risk score that documents the consequence of inaction (regulatory escalation, revenue loss rate, etc.) with evidence and a time horizon.
rule.rationale: Without the status-quo option, the strategy is advocacy; the steering committee cannot see what happens if they defer. A scored status-quo with a time-bounded risk is the most persuasive element of the document for forcing a decision.

section.name: sign-off-and-lifecycle
section.p: The change strategy recommendation must be signed by the decision-maker before downstream SDD work begins. Sign-off carries: signed_by, signed_at, option chosen, and rationale. Each revision is a git commit with Conventional Commits format; the change log is the rationale audit trail.

Human-in-the-loop checkpoints for the full strategy analysis arc: (a) need statement signed by sponsor; (b) future state locked by exec sponsor; (c) change strategy recommendation signed by decision-maker; (d) 90-day post-decision review where the BA and faion-improver compare predicted gaps closed vs. actual.

After the change strategy is signed, the faion-sdd-executor-agent generates the SDD task tree (constitution, spec, design, implementation-plan) inside .aidocs/backlog/ for the chosen path. The faion-feature-executor then executes the discrete tasks, each carrying strategy_id for traceability back to the gap and need.

section.name: agentic-workflow
section.p: Run a faion-brainstorm divergence pass on options before the change-strategy-agent generates the evaluation set; without it, the option set collapses to the three vendors who replied loudest or the three options the sponsor mentioned in passing. The divergence pass surfaces non-obvious options (e.g. phased buy + build, consortium with peer orgs, regulatory sandbox exemption).

Run an opus change-strategy-agent that generates build / buy / partner / modify / status-quo options against the gap-priority map, each with a cost / time / risk / capability profile. Then hand the option set to decision-analysis (sibling methodology) for weighted-matrix selection. The BA hands the option set to decision-analysis; the decision-analysis methodology selects; only the human signs.

Recommended subagents: faion-brainstorm (divergence pass on options, most commonly skipped), faion-sdd-executor-agent (generates SDD task tree for the chosen path), faion-feature-executor (executes discrete tasks from the chosen change strategy), faion-improver (quarterly meta-loop comparing predicted vs. actual gap closure).

section.name: ai-agent-gotchas
section.p: Change strategy as a single option: agents skip option generation when the sponsor signals a preference. Hard-prompt: "Generate at least 3 options plus status-quo regardless of any preference expressed in the input. Do not pre-select."
Order bias on options: whichever change-strategy option is mentioned first or last gets favored in the agent's narrative. Randomize option order in every evaluation prompt.

Wrong-agent invocation: a feature-executor should never make a strategy decision; it should fail-closed and ping the BA. Only change-strategy-agent produces option sets; only decision-analysis selects; only the human signs.
Bulk transitions are dangerous: a runaway agent that rewrites 30 option rows is hard to undo. Cap any agent action to N rows per run; require human review on the diff.

Prompt-injected vendor copy: when options include external vendor input, vendors can plant favorable framing. Strip suspicious instructions; score all options from primary sources (published pricing, audited case studies, the org's own TCO data).

section.name: tools
section.p: CLI tools: pulp (Python linear programming) when budget and capability constraints turn change-strategy options into hard constraints; pandas plus numpy for aggregating option profiles and weighted-matrix computation; streamlit for live future-state / gap workshop UI where sponsors change targets in real time and see updated gap scores; pandoc for rendering the locked strategy doc to PDF or DOCX for sponsor sign-off; mermaid-cli for the change-strategy decision tree.

Services: Confluence or Notion for the locked strategy document; Jira or Linear for one epic per gap with status tracking; Avolution ABACUS for multi-criteria scoring of change-strategy options inside an EA model; Streamlit or Hex for workshop-grade re-rank UI when sponsors change targets in real time.

list.item: Validate with stakeholders: present the signed change strategy to all stakeholder groups identified in the coverage matrix.
Develop business case: the signed change strategy is the primary input; it carries the option chosen, the evaluation rationale, and the risk profile.
Get executive approval: the decision-maker signature is the gate; downstream commitments are fragile without it.
Begin detailed requirements: connect to Requirements Lifecycle methodology (sibling). The faion-sdd-executor-agent generates the SDD task tree inside .aidocs/backlog/ for the chosen path.
Schedule 90-day review: set a calendar entry at sign-off time; the BA and faion-improver compare predicted gaps closed vs. actual and log calibration errors in .aidocs/memory/patterns.md.

section.name: references
section.p: IIBA — BABOK Guide v3, Knowledge Area 6, section 6.4 Define Change Strategy. Christensen — The Innovator's Dilemma (when to disrupt vs. sustain; informs build/buy/partner choice). Treacy & Wiersema — The Discipline of Market Leaders (option archetypes). Hammond, Keeney, Raiffa — Smart Choices (decision quality discipline; pairs with the decision-analysis sibling). Sibling: pro/ba/business-analyst/decision-analysis — option-selection step inside change strategy. Sibling: pro/ba/business-analyst/requirements-lifecycle — picks up after change strategy sign-off.
